decks.title: Lecture 1: Course Introduction and Product Management

cards.definition: A name, symbol, or combination thereof that differentiates a product and carries dimensions built through consumer perceptions, expectations, and experiences; more than a product.
The intrinsic value a brand holds over a generic or unbranded equivalent, arising from consumer loyalty, perceived quality, and brand associations.
The organizational advocate responsible for cultivating brand image, building brand equity, and sustaining brand worth over time.
The third phase of the Crawford NPD process, in which candidate product concepts are assessed for technical, market, and financial viability before committing to full development.
A category of new product that delivers similar performance to an existing product at lower cost, often through redesigned production processes.
A management role requiring engagement with multiple organizational functions — marketing, engineering, design, finance — simultaneously; the defining characteristic of the Product Manager role.
The six-phase product development model comprising Planning, Concept Development, System-Level Design, Detail Design, Testing and Refinement, and Production Ramp-Up, separated by formal review gates.
A product that takes a company into a market category new to that firm, though not necessarily new to the world.
An invention that creates an entirely new market category; the highest-risk, highest-reward form of new product innovation.

The structured sequence — Opportunity Identification, Concept Generation, Concept/Project Evaluation, Development, Launch — through which an organization conceives, designs, and commercializes a new product.
The first phase of the Crawford NPD process, in which the firm identifies and selects the market opportunities and strategic directions most worth pursuing.
Anything that can be offered to a market for attention, acquisition, use, or consumption that might satisfy a need or want, including physical goods, services, and experiences.
The sequence of activities an organization undertakes to conceive, design, and commercialize a product; encompasses intellectual and organizational activities, not merely physical production.
The cross-functional leader who drives the vision, strategy, design, and execution of a product and bears responsibility for its commercial success or failure.
A product development model in which the Design-Build-Test cycle loops repeatedly before production ramp-up, enabling faster learning and adaptation.
A new product strategy in which an existing product is retargeted for a new use, user group, or market segment without necessarily changing the physical product.

A product development model depicted as concentric rings, where each revolution represents increasing fidelity and cost, enabling risk reduction through iterative validation at progressively greater investment.

A framework stating a successful innovation must satisfy three conditions simultaneously: desirability to people, technical feasibility, and business viability; depicted as a Venn diagram with the sweet spot at the center.

decks.title: Lecture 2: New Product Development Part 1 — Opportunity Identification and Concept Generation

cards.definition: Phase 2 of the NPD process in which the team creates new product ideas through external and internal search, grounded in identified customer needs; the most fruitful ideation identifies both problems and solutions simultaneously.
A five-step process for identifying, interpreting, and prioritizing customer needs: scope definition, data gathering, needs-statement writing, importance quantification, and process reflection.

A qualitative research method in which researchers observe and participate in customers' natural environments to surface behaviors, workarounds, and frustrations that customers cannot articulate in interviews or surveys.
Customer needs that are easy to express, widely known and understood, and likely already fulfilled by existing products in the market.

A framework for evaluating industry competitive dynamics through five forces: threat of new entrants, bargaining power of buyers, bargaining power of suppliers, threat of substitute products, and intensity of rivalry among existing competitors.
A project planning tool that displays NPD tasks as horizontal bars across a timeline, indicating start dates, durations, deliverable due dates, and periods of continued refinement.

Customer needs that customers may not be aware of and cannot express; when addressed, they can create entirely new market categories and exceed expectations. Identified primarily through ethnographic observation.
The process of estimating total potential revenue for a product, structured as TAM, SAM, and SOM, using a four-step guesstimation approach.

A structured translation of a raw customer quote into product-requirement language that expresses what the product should do, is specific, positive, framed as a product attribute, and avoids "must" or "should."
An idea for a new product in embryonic form — a newly sensed need, newly discovered technology, or rough match between a need and a possible solution, typically described in less than one page.

A risk mitigation strategy in which a large pool of raw opportunities is passed through successive evaluation filters, with only the highest-quality opportunities surviving to receive development investment.

A macro-environment scanning framework assessing Political, Economic, Social, Technological, Environmental, and Legal factors that affect market attractiveness and opportunity viability, rated on a 1–5 favorability scale.
A guiding document that defines the product description, benefit proposition, key business goals, primary and secondary markets, assumptions, and stakeholders for a new product development project.
A technical, measurable definition of what a product must do to satisfy a customer need, expressed in engineering metrics with a target value. Translates needs statements into design requirements.
Two contrasting opportunity-sourcing approaches: Push originates from internal capabilities or passion; Pull is driven by identified market needs pulling solutions from the firm.

A three-question opportunity evaluation framework: Is it Real? (genuine market need exists), Can we Win? (competitive advantage is achievable), Is it Worth It? (acceptable financial return at acceptable risk).

The share of the Serviceable Addressable Market that a firm can realistically capture given competition and execution constraints; the most practically relevant market size figure for resource allocation.
The entire theoretical revenue opportunity for a product in a specific market assuming 100% market share; represents the maximum possible size of the opportunity.
Customer needs that are easy to express and widely acknowledged, but known to be difficult to address and generally not yet satisfied by any existing product.

decks.title: Lecture 3: New Product Development Part 2 — Concept Screening and Testing

cards.definition: Evaluating competitive products against customer needs and objective metrics to set realistic target specification values.
A matrix tool for rapid, qualitative comparison of multiple product concepts against a reference concept using a +/0/– scale. Net score = Plusses minus Minuses.
A weighted matrix tool that rates concepts on a 1–5 numerical scale across importance-weighted criteria, producing a total weighted score for ranking and selection.

The phase in which selected product concepts are validated against customer and strategic criteria to eliminate concepts that are technically unfeasible, strategically misaligned, or lacking market fit.
A formally structured, product-attribute expression of what the customer requires, written in positive "what not how" language without solution prescription or obligatory phrasing.
A strategy of committing to final specifications early in development, enabling downstream tasks to begin with firm inputs and facilitating cost and performance optimisation.
The best achievable value for a product specification metric, representing the target if no constraints existed; contrasted with the Marginal Value.
A customer insight framework asserting that people "hire" products to accomplish a specific job in a specific situation: "When [situation], I want to [motivation], so I can [desired outcome]."
A strategy of committing to final specifications as late as possible in development, allowing better alignment with evolving customer needs and competitive dynamics.
The minimum acceptable value for a product specification metric — the threshold below which the product fails to satisfy the need.
The measurable property used to operationalise a customer need as a product specification; combined with a value and units to form a complete specification.

A formal document defining a product's description, benefit proposition, key business goals, primary and secondary markets, constraints, assumptions, and stakeholders; used to scope customer needs analysis.
A framework representing a product concept as the intersection of at least two of three inputs — customer Need/Benefit, physical Form, and enabling Technology. A new product requires all three.
A verbal or prototype statement describing what will change in a product and how the customer stands to gain or lose; requires at least two of Need, Form, and Technology to be feasible.
The quantified, measurable translation of a customer needs statement into engineering terms; composed of Metric + Value(s) + Units.

The baseline concept used as the comparison standard in Concept Screening and Scoring Matrices; typically the current/existing product; assigned a score of 0 (screening) or 3 (scoring) on all criteria.

The raw, verbatim language in which customers express their needs, frustrations, and desires, gathered through interviews, focus groups, observation, and ethnography; the starting input to customer needs translation.
The product of a criterion's importance weight and a concept's rating on that criterion in the Concept Scoring Matrix; summed across all criteria to produce the Total Score.

decks.title: Lecture 4: Concept Testing Review and Guest Lecture

cards.definition: A controlled experiment releasing two product versions simultaneously to different user groups to compare performance; used at or near production release for optimization decisions.
Internal performance and user interface testing conducted by company personnel on a near-complete product to identify functional failures before external exposure.
Validation and acceptance testing conducted by real external users in realistic conditions to confirm the product meets expectations prior to full production ramp-up.

A structured group ideation method governed by rules such as deferring judgment, building on others' ideas, and encouraging wild ideas, used during concept generation to maximize variety and creativity.
The process of merging strong attributes from multiple evaluated concepts into a superior hybrid concept, rather than accepting the highest-scoring concept as-is.
A quantitative decision tool that multiplies criteria weights by concept ratings and sums the products to rank competing concepts; used after concept screening for finer concept selection.
An earlier, coarser evaluation method using a +/0/- scale to compare concepts against a reference concept and eliminate clearly inferior options before the scoring matrix is applied.

Phase 3 of the NPD process in which surviving product concepts are evaluated through consumer research, technical assessment, and strategic alignment checks to determine which should move into development.
The assignment of numerical importance scores to each selection criterion, derived from consumer problem surveys, that determine how much each criterion influences the total concept score.
A structured checkpoint between phases of the product development pipeline where management decides whether sufficient evidence exists to commit resources to the next phase.

A strategic document defining the boundaries of acceptable product concepts for the firm, used during concept testing to eliminate concepts outside the firm's technology, market knowledge, innovation level, or competitive strategy.
Iterative physical or digital design testing during system-level and detail design phases to evaluate how well a prototype meets specifications before user testing begins.
Early-stage consumer research exposing concept descriptions to target users to assess comprehension, desirability, and purchase intent before any prototype is built.
Statistical consumer research conducted in the Testing and Refinement phase to measure demand, preference share, and purchase probability at scale.
The baseline product against which all other concepts are rated in a concept screening matrix, typically the current market leader or current company offering.
The process of varying uncertain criteria weights or ratings in the scoring matrix to determine whether the concept ranking is robust or would change under plausible alternative assumptions.
Benchmarking of a concept or prototype against established technical specifications, typically conducted in the early stages of system-level design.
The product of a criterion's importance weight and a concept's raw rating on that criterion; the sum of all weighted scores for a concept constitutes its total score in the matrix.

decks.title: Lecture 5: Conjoint Analysis for Product Design

cards.definition: The assumption that a product's total utility is the sum of the part-worth utilities of its individual attribute levels, with no cross-attribute interaction effects.
An important product characteristic that drives consumer choice in conjoint analysis. Each attribute has two or more levels.
The reference level of an attribute omitted from OLS regression and against which all other levels are compared. Its utility is set to zero by convention and captured in the intercept.
A conjoint variant in which respondents choose their most preferred option from a set of competing profiles rather than rating or ranking. More closely mirrors real purchasing behavior.
A survey-based statistical technique that determines how consumers value different product attributes by presenting product profiles and analyzing stated preferences through regression.
A supply chain strategy in which a product is manufactured to a semi-finished state and held as a generic subassembly; the differentiating step is performed as late as possible in the supply network.
An attribute that is both important to consumers and varies meaningfully across competing products, making it a key driver of actual choice behavior.
A binary (0/1) indicator variable used in OLS regression to represent whether a specific attribute level is present in a given profile. One level per attribute is omitted as the baseline.
A conjoint design in which only a statistically chosen subset of all attribute-level combinations is shown to respondents, reducing survey length while allowing unbiased estimation of main effects.
A conjoint study design in which respondents evaluate every possible combination of attribute levels. The number of profiles equals the product of the number of levels across all attributes.
The specific values or categories that an attribute can take in a conjoint study.
A production strategy combining the efficiency of mass production with individual customization, typically enabled by modular product architecture and postponement.
A product architecture in which functional elements are grouped into self-contained modules that can be independently designed, produced, and swapped with minimal effect on other modules.
The statistical method used in conjoint analysis to estimate part-worth utilities by regressing preference scores on dummy-coded attribute level variables to produce coefficient estimates.
The incremental value in preference units that a consumer places on a specific attribute level, estimated as the OLS regression coefficient on the corresponding dummy variable.
A Japanese fail-proofing design philosophy that builds physical or procedural safeguards into a product or process to prevent user errors before they occur.
The arrangement of functional elements of a product into physical or logical chunks that serve as building blocks for a product or family of products.
A single product concept in a conjoint study, defined by one specific level from each attribute. Respondents evaluate profiles to reveal their underlying preferences.
The percentage contribution of each attribute to total preference variation. Calculated as (attribute utility range / sum of all attribute utility ranges) × 100.
The maximum dollar amount a consumer would pay for an attribute upgrade, derived from conjoint results by scaling an attribute's utility gain by the price sensitivity coefficient.

decks.title: Lecture 6: Market Experiment and Project Management

cards.definition: The simplest form of randomized controlled experiment. Subjects are randomly split into a control group (A) and a treatment group (B), a single variable is manipulated for group B, and outcome variables are compared.
The smallest unit of work effort consuming both time and resources that a project manager can schedule and control; a node in the AON network diagram.
The maximum time an activity can be delayed without delaying overall project completion. Calculated as S = LS − ES (or LF − EF). Activities on the critical path have S = 0.
A network diagram format in which activities are represented as nodes and precedence relationships as directed arrows. Each node stores ES, EF, LS, LF, and activity duration.
A relationship in which manipulation of variable X directly produces a change in variable Y, independent of all other factors. Distinct from correlation, which may be confounded by third variables.
A range within which the true population mean is estimated to lie with 95% probability. Non-overlapping confidence intervals between two groups indicate a statistically significant difference.
Variables that affect both the treatment variable and the outcome variable, creating spurious associations. Random assignment in RCTs eliminates confounding in expectation.
The hypothetical state of the world in which a different treatment was applied. The fundamental challenge of causal inference is that counterfactuals are unobservable.
A project scheduling technique using the network diagram to identify the critical path, calculate ES/EF/LS/LF for all activities, determine minimum project duration, and calculate slack times.
An activity that lies on the critical path, has zero slack, and whose delay directly delays the entire project.
The longest path through the project network from Start to Finish. It determines the minimum possible project duration. All activities on the critical path have zero slack.
The earliest time an activity can be completed, calculated as EF = ES + t. Determined during the forward pass through the network.
The earliest time an activity can begin, equal to the maximum EF of all immediate predecessors. For activities with no predecessors, ES = 0.
A cognitive bias in which individuals assign higher value to objects they own than to identical objects they do not own. Relevant to product trials, free samples, and loss aversion in pricing.

An experimental design that simultaneously tests all combinations of levels across two or more treatment dimensions. Reveals optimal combinations and interaction effects that sequential A/B testing cannot find.
The latest time an activity can be completed without delaying the project. Determined during the backward pass as the minimum LS of all immediate successors.
The latest time an activity can begin without delaying the project. Calculated as LS = LF − t during the backward pass.
A graphical display of project activities and their precedence relationships. Consists of nodes (activities) and directed arcs (dependencies). Used as the basis for CPM/PERT analysis.
A sequence of activities in the network diagram leading from the Start node to the Finish node. Path length is the sum of activity durations along that route.
A project scheduling and network analysis technique functionally equivalent to CPM. Historically developed for large-scale government and defense projects.
A constraint specifying that one activity cannot begin until one or more preceding activities have been completed. Represented as directed arrows in the AON diagram.
A unique, one-time set of interrelated activities with a definite start and end point, designed to achieve specific objectives within a limited time frame using a defined allocation of resources.

The deliberate shortening of one or more critical path activities by committing additional resources to reduce overall project duration. The minimum-cost approach crashes the cheapest critical activity first.

An experiment in which subjects are randomly assigned to a control or treatment group. The gold standard for causal inference because random assignment distributes confounding variables equally across groups in expectation.

A statistical method for estimating the relationship between an outcome variable (Y) and explanatory variables (X). In Y = a + bX, b represents the treatment effect and its p-value tests statistical significance.
See Activity Slack (S). The amount of time an activity can be delayed without delaying overall project completion.
A result is statistically significant if the observed difference between groups is unlikely to have occurred by chance, assessed via non-overlapping 95% confidence intervals or a p-value below 0.05.

The causal impact of the manipulated variable (X) on the outcome variable (Y), isolated from all other influences through random assignment. In regression Y = a + bX, coefficient b estimates the treatment effect.

A hierarchical decomposition of all work required to complete a project. Level 0 is the overall project; Level 1 represents major work streams; Level 2 and below represent individual schedulable activities.
The strongest form of brand resonance; consumers willingly invest time, energy, money, or other resources in the brand beyond purchase or consumption.
A cognitive model that views memory as a network of nodes and connecting links; brand knowledge is the set of brand-related nodes and their associations in this network.
A dimension of brand resonance in which consumers view the brand as something special beyond mere functional utility; characterized by love or devotion.
A name, term, sign, symbol, or design, or combination thereof, intended to identify the goods/services of one seller and differentiate them from competitors (AMA definition).
The strength of the brand node in consumer memory; the extent to which consumers can recognize or recall the brand under different conditions. Composed of brand recognition and brand recall.
A dimension of brand judgment capturing perceived expertise, trustworthiness, and likability; drives the extent to which consumers believe the brand can deliver on its promises.
The identifiable components of a brand — name, logo, symbol, packaging, slogan, characters — chosen to identify and differentiate the brand and contribute to brand knowledge building.
The value added to a product or service by its brand, as determined by consumer perception, experience, and differential response relative to an unbranded equivalent.
Customers' emotional responses and reactions to a brand relating to its social currency; the six types are warmth, fun, excitement, security, social approval, and self-respect.
The clear and solid meaning a marketer provides for a brand — communicating who the product is, what it does, and why consumers should care.
Consumers' perceptions about a brand as reflected by the associations held in consumer memory; the totality of all brand-related associations in the associative network.
The extrinsic, intangible properties of a product or service linked to a brand through user profiles, purchase/usage situations, personality/values, and history/heritage/experiences.
Customers' personal opinions and evaluations of a brand synthesized from performance and imagery associations; types include quality, credibility, consideration, and superiority.
The full set of brand associations in consumer memory, comprising brand awareness and brand image; the key source of brand equity under the CBBE framework.
A short, three-to-five-word phrase capturing the essential spirit of brand positioning; structured around an emotional modifier, descriptive modifier, and brand function. Guides internal decisions.
How a product or service meets customers' functional needs; includes primary/secondary features, reliability/durability/serviceability, service quality, style and design, and price.
The act of designing the company's offer and image so that it occupies a distinct and valued place in target customers' minds; defined from the consumer's benefit perspective.
Consumers' ability to retrieve a brand from memory when given the product category, needs fulfilled, or a purchase situation — an unprompted form of brand awareness.
Consumers' ability to confirm prior exposure to a brand when given the brand as a cue — a prompted form of brand awareness.

The ultimate relationship and level of identification a customer has with a brand; characterized by depth of psychological bond across behavioral loyalty, attitudinal attachment, sense of community, and active engagement.

How easily and often the brand is evoked under various situations; measures depth and breadth of brand awareness and links the brand to its category and usage situations. Forms the base of the CBBE pyramid.

Keller's framework defining brand equity as the differential effect of brand knowledge on consumer response to marketing, structured as a four-level pyramid: Salience → Performance/Imagery → Judgments/Feelings → Resonance.
Attributes or benefits consumers strongly associate with a brand and believe they could not find to the same extent with a competitive brand; the basis of differentiation in positioning.
Associations not necessarily unique to the brand but shared with other brands; necessary to establish category credibility before differentiation can be effective.
A dimension of brand resonance in which consumers feel a sense of kinship or affiliation with other users of the brand or members of the brand community.

decks.title: Lecture 8: Brand Sustainability and Personal Branding

cards.definition: The weakening of a brand's distinctive associations, perceived quality, or exclusivity, typically from overextension into inappropriate categories, excessive licensing, or overly broad distribution.

The use of an established brand name to enter a new product category, leveraging existing equity to reduce consumer adoption risk; carries dilution risk if the new category is incongruent with the parent brand's identity.

A short, three-to-five-word phrase capturing the essential meaning and spirit of a brand, used internally to guide decision-making and ensure consistency. Format: [Emotional Modifier] + [Descriptive Modifier] + [Brand Function].
The deliberate strategic effort to change how a target audience perceives a brand by altering its associations along key perceptual dimensions; can be incremental or disruptive in scope.

The use of luxury or premium goods purchases to communicate social status, wealth, taste, and group membership to others — the mechanism by which conspicuous consumption generates social utility for the purchaser.

In the Three D's framework, the dimension assessing whether an individual can consistently and credibly deliver the value promised by their brand positioning, grounded in genuine skills and demonstrated track record.
In the Three D's framework, the dimension assessing whether a positioning reflects genuine personal passion and whether the target audience wants what the individual stands for.
In the Three D's framework, the dimension assessing whether a positioning is sufficiently distinctive and sustainable to carve out a unique market position that competitors cannot easily replicate.
The strategic vulnerability arising from excessive dependence on consumers from a single country or region, making brand revenue sensitive to localized political, economic, or logistical disruptions.

A visual representation of the associations — attributes, emotions, symbols, people, and situations — that a target audience links to a brand; used to audit brand equity and identify positioning gaps.

The evolved luxury consumption model characterized by experiential engagement, digital-first access, cultural resonance, intergenerational relevance, and a shift from pure status symbolism toward self-expression and values alignment.
A two-dimensional grid plotting brands on key consumer perception axes to visualize competitive positioning, identify white spaces, and design repositioning strategies.

The sum of associations, perceptions, and reputation that a professional intentionally cultivates in the minds of a defined target audience, managed using the same strategic frameworks as corporate brand management.

Attributes or associations unique to a brand and strongly linked to it — the distinctive elements giving consumers a reason to prefer that brand over alternatives. Must satisfy Desirability, Deliverability, and Differentiation criteria.

The deliberate limitation of supply or access to a luxury product, used to preserve exclusivity, maintain high price points, and reinforce the signaling value of ownership. Exists in tension with revenue maximization and sustainability goals.
An individual participation component (5% of grade) in which each student briefly pitches an original product idea to the class.

Any misrepresentation with intent to deceive, failure to acknowledge sources, falsification of information, cheating at examinations, or inappropriate use of resources — all prohibited under NUS policy.
The long-term cumulative value embedded in a brand, built through consistent strategic choices and consumer associations over time.
The long-term vitality and consumer relevance of a brand, contrasted with short-term performance metrics. Decisions that boost immediate results must be balanced against sustaining brand health.
The practice of crafting and communicating a coherent narrative around a brand or product to build consumer connection and meaning.
The structured process of assessing new product ideas against market, technical, and financial criteria before committing to full development; covered in Week 3.

A quantitative research technique that measures how consumers trade off among product attributes to reveal preference structures and implied willingness to pay; used in NPD to optimize product design.
Quantitative and experimental methods for projecting consumer demand and market response before a product's full commercial launch; covered in Week 6.
A structured, human-centered approach to innovation emphasizing empathy with users, iterative prototyping, and creative problem-solving.
The written deliverable of the group project, worth 20% of the final grade, documenting the full new product launch strategy developed by each team.
relatedTerms: Group Project on New Product Launching

cards.term: Group Project on New Product Launching
cards.definition: The primary applied assessment in MKT3717, comprising group presentations (25%) and a final marketing plan report (20%), requiring teams to develop and present a complete new product launch strategy.
An approach to product and service design that places end-user needs, behaviors, and experiences at the center of the innovation process.
The quantitative exercise of estimating the total addressable market (TAM) or relevant opportunity size for a new product; practiced as a hands-on exercise in Week 2.
The full end-to-end process by which firms move from identifying customer needs to designing, testing, launching, and managing new products; the organizing spine of the first half of the course.
A structured assessment component (5% of grade) in which students formally evaluate their group project teammates' contributions.
relatedTerms: Group Project on New Product Launching

cards.definition: Taking someone else's work or ideas and presenting them as one's own; strictly prohibited under NUS academic integrity policy.
The scheme by which the functional elements of a product are arranged and interact; covered in Week 3 as part of concept development and prototyping.
The stages a product passes through from introduction through growth, maturity, and decline; the course's managerial perspective involves actively managing products across all these stages.
The activity of planning and organizing a service's components — people, infrastructure, communication, and material — to improve quality and user experience.

decks.title: Practice Problems: Final Test Study Guide

cards.definition: A controlled market experiment assigning participants randomly to a control version (A) and treatment version (B) to compare outcome metrics and establish causal impact of a product, feature, price, or message change.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Final Test Study Guide

cards.definition: A name, term, sign, symbol, design, or combination thereof intended to identify the goods or services of one seller and differentiate them from competitors.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Final Test Study Guide

cards.definition: The added value a brand name confers on a product beyond its functional benefits, reflected in consumer perceptions, preferences, and behaviors.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Final Test Study Guide

cards.definition: The highest level of Keller's Brand Resonance Pyramid: a deep psychological relationship between consumer and brand expressed through behavioral loyalty, attitudinal attachment, sense of community, and active engagement.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Final Test Study Guide

cards.definition: A quantitative concept selection tool that multiplies consumer-derived importance weights by concept ratings on each criterion and sums the products for a total score per concept.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Final Test Study Guide

cards.definition: A coarse concept selection tool using a +/0/− scale relative to a reference concept to eliminate clearly inferior alternatives before applying the more detailed scoring matrix.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Final Test Study Guide

cards.definition: A quantitative concept testing technique that decomposes overall consumer preference into part-worth contributions of each product attribute and level via regression; used to identify important attributes and simulate preference.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Final Test Study Guide

cards.definition: The sequence of activities from project start to finish that takes the longest time to complete, determining minimum total project duration; all activities on the critical path have zero slack.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Final Test Study Guide

cards.definition: An experimental design that tests all combinations of multiple independent variables simultaneously, enabling estimation of main effects and interaction effects; more efficient than running separate A/B tests for each factor.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Final Test Study Guide

cards.definition: A customer research philosophy framing needs as functional, social, or emotional jobs consumers are trying to accomplish, revealing latent needs and non-obvious competitors more effectively than attribute-focused surveys.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Final Test Study Guide

cards.definition: A four-level brand building model: Brand Salience → Brand Performance + Brand Imagery → Brand Judgments + Brand Feelings → Brand Resonance; each level must be built before the next can be achieved.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Final Test Study Guide

cards.definition: A new product that is an addition to an existing product line of the firm, designed to broaden the portfolio for the firm's current markets.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Final Test Study Guide

cards.definition: A product architecture composed of standardized, interchangeable modules combined in different configurations to produce variety without redesigning from scratch; enables mass customization and reduces inventory risk under demand uncertainty.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Final Test Study Guide

cards.definition: A graphical representation of project activities and precedence relationships used to identify the critical path, compute slack times, and make crashing decisions via forward pass and backward pass calculations.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Final Test Study Guide

cards.definition: The contribution of a specific attribute level to a consumer's overall product preference, estimated as a regression coefficient in conjoint analysis; used to calculate attribute importance and simulate preference.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Final Test Study Guide

cards.definition: A framework for evaluating industry competitive attractiveness through five structural forces: Threat of New Entrants, Bargaining Power of Buyers, Bargaining Power of Suppliers, Threat of Substitutes, and Intensity of Rivalry.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Final Test Study Guide

cards.definition: The deliberate reduction of activity durations by adding resources to shorten total project duration at additional cost; prioritizes the critical path activity with the lowest crash cost per unit of time saved.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Final Test Study Guide

cards.definition: A three-question opportunity evaluation framework: Is it Real? (genuine need + technical achievability), Can We Win? (sustainable competitive advantage), Is it Worth It? (sufficient financial return at acceptable risk).
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Final Test Study Guide

cards.definition: The amount of time a project activity can be delayed without delaying total project completion; computed as LS − ES or LF − EF; activities on the critical path have slack = 0.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Final Test Study Guide

cards.definition: Three nested market sizing estimates: Total Addressable Market (100% share), Serviceable Addressable Market (realistic target segment), and Serviceable Obtainable Market (credibly capturable share given competition); SOM is the operationally relevant figure.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Final Test Study Guide

cards.definition: A hierarchical decomposition of a project into progressively smaller components and individual work packages, serving as the input for constructing a project network diagram and identifying precedence relationships.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Final Test Study Guide

decks.title: Supplementary Reading: Slack Launch Strategy — From 0 to $1B

cards.definition: The moment a user or team crosses a product-specific usage threshold that predicts sustained retention. For Slack, activation is defined as a team exchanging 2,000 messages.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Slack Launch Strategy — From 0 to $1B

cards.definition: A B2B go-to-market motion in which a product enters organizations through individual teams at a price point not requiring senior management approval, spreading organically before formal enterprise procurement begins.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Slack Launch Strategy — From 0 to $1B

cards.definition: Paul Buchheit's framework stating that excellence in a few critical dimensions is more valuable than broad adequacy across many features.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Slack Launch Strategy — From 0 to $1B

cards.definition: The go-to-market challenge faced by products in markets where prospective customers do not yet recognize the problem the product solves, requiring the company to establish category legitimacy before selling its solution.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Slack Launch Strategy — From 0 to $1B

cards.definition: Slack's organizational unit combining QA and customer support into a single team responsible for processing feedback, routing it to product teams, and resolving user issues — treated as a strategic asset rather than a cost center.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Slack Launch Strategy — From 0 to $1B

cards.definition: The number of unique users engaging with a product on a given day, measuring genuine engagement distinct from total registered accounts or teams created.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Slack Launch Strategy — From 0 to $1B

cards.definition: The plan a company executes to bring a product to market, encompassing positioning, pricing, distribution, launch tactics, customer acquisition, and retention.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Slack Launch Strategy — From 0 to $1B

cards.definition: Slack's cross-device synchronization feature that tracks exactly where each user left off in every conversation across all devices in real time, eliminating context loss when switching between devices.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Slack Launch Strategy — From 0 to $1B

cards.definition: A product-specific activation threshold, determined empirically from cohort retention data, at which a team's probability of long-term retention becomes highly predictable. Slack's magic number is 2,000 messages, after which 93% of teams remain active.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Slack Launch Strategy — From 0 to $1B

cards.definition: The go-to-market activity of teaching prospective customers that a product category exists and that they have the problem it solves — required when a significant portion of the target market does not recognize the category.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Slack Launch Strategy — From 0 to $1B

cards.definition: A product category that is new or undefined, in which customers have not yet established buying criteria, recognized solution providers, or standard evaluation processes, requiring buyer education before persuasion.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Slack Launch Strategy — From 0 to $1B

cards.definition: A framing choice for a controlled early-access product release that signals quality and intentionality, as opposed to calling the same release a "beta," which connotes instability and incompleteness.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Slack Launch Strategy — From 0 to $1B

cards.definition: Non-numerical user insights gathered through support tickets, social media mentions, interview responses, and open-ended feedback — treated as equally important to quantitative metrics.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Slack Launch Strategy — From 0 to $1B

cards.definition: The rate at which users or teams continue using a product over time after activation. Slack's 93% retention rate among teams crossing the 2,000-message threshold was the business case for investing in activation.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Slack Launch Strategy — From 0 to $1B

cards.definition: The dynamic in collaborative software where a single team member who refuses to use the product can prevent the entire team from adopting it, making it qualitatively different from individual-user product adoption.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Slack Launch Strategy — From 0 to $1B

cards.definition: Slack's curated collection of enthusiastic, unsolicited public tweets from users expressing extreme satisfaction — used as social proof and a symbol of organic word-of-mouth advocacy.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Slack Launch Strategy — From 0 to $1B

cards.definition: Organic product awareness and adoption driven by users spontaneously recommending or publicly praising a product to their networks, without paid promotion.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Slack Launch Strategy — From 0 to $1B

cards.definition: A public figure who represents and embodies a brand's values in public communications, used to signal repositioning toward target consumer segments.
The weakening of a brand's equity or premium positioning caused by over-extension of the product line, inappropriate distribution, or associations inconsistent with core brand identity.
The commercial and perceptual value added to a product by its brand name, associations, and consumer loyalty — enabling premium pricing, preference, and advocacy beyond functional attributes alone.

A strategic process of restoring a brand's relevance, desirability, and commercial performance after decline, typically involving changes to creative direction, target consumer, product range, and communications.

Gucci's ten-year strategic charter embedding environmental sustainability, employee welfare, community engagement, and production efficiency as core organisational commitments, tracked publicly via the Gucci Equilibrium platform.
A go-to-market model in which a brand sells directly to end consumers through its own stores rather than wholesale intermediaries, giving control over pricing, experience, data, and margin.

Clothing and accessories designed to be worn across the gender spectrum without masculine or feminine coding. Gucci normalised this under Alessandro Michele, aligning with Gen Z and millennial identity trends.

An online platform launched in 2018 that publicly tracks Gucci's real-time progress against its sustainability commitments, functioning as a transparency mechanism and authenticity signal for socially conscious consumers.

The French luxury conglomerate formerly known as PPR that owns Gucci, Saint Laurent, Balenciaga, and others. In 2019 it operated in 120+ countries with €15.8B revenue; Gucci contributed 63% of revenues and 78% of profits.

The expansion of an existing brand into a new product category adjacent to its core offering. The strategic risk is that categories with different price points or positioning may dilute the parent brand's equity.
An integrated retailing approach providing consumers a seamless brand experience across all purchase channels — physical stores, branded e-commerce, third-party online, social media, and mobile.

The luxury market segment comprising fashion, leather goods, shoes, jewellery, watches, and fragrances and beauty products that are personally consumed. This segment was worth €281 billion globally in 2019.
A brand strategy positioning a brand around a higher social, environmental, or cultural mission beyond product attributes, resonating with consumers who expect brands to address societal challenges.

The resale and repurchase of pre-owned luxury goods via platforms such as TheRealReal and Farfetch. The second-hand luxury market reached €26B in 2019, growing four times faster than the primary market.
Gucci's internal advisory body of its most talented employees under 30, created by CEO Marco Bizzarri to provide generational consumer insights to senior leadership.

A merchandising framework dividing a brand's assortment into stable core products (Gucci: 70%) and trend-driven items (Gucci: 30%). Staples provide revenue stability; trendy items generate media coverage and new consumer acquisition.

decks.title: Practice Problems: Conjoint Analysis Practice Problems

cards.definition: The assumption that a consumer's total utility for a product equals the simple sum of utilities derived from each individual attribute level, with no interaction effects between attributes.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Conjoint Analysis Practice Problems

cards.definition: A product characteristic that meaningfully differentiates alternatives in a product category, described in discrete and unambiguous terms.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Conjoint Analysis Practice Problems

cards.definition: The omitted reference category for a given attribute in conjoint regression. All other levels' part-worths are measured relative to the baseline, which is assigned a part-worth of zero.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Conjoint Analysis Practice Problems

cards.definition: A survey-based quantitative technique that determines how consumers value different product attributes by presenting product profiles and analyzing ratings, rankings, or choices via regression.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Conjoint Analysis Practice Problems

cards.definition: A binary (0/1) indicator variable used in regression to represent whether a profile contains a specific attribute level. For an attribute with k levels, k − 1 dummy variables are required.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Conjoint Analysis Practice Problems

cards.definition: A conjoint study design in which only a carefully selected subset of all possible attribute level combinations is presented to respondents, used when full factorial designs would create too many profiles.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Conjoint Analysis Practice Problems

cards.definition: A conjoint study design in which respondents evaluate every possible combination of attribute levels. Total profiles equals the product of the number of levels across all attributes.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Conjoint Analysis Practice Problems

cards.definition: A specific value or category that an attribute can take.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Conjoint Analysis Practice Problems

cards.definition: A preference elicitation method in which respondents assign rank numbers to product profiles, producing an ordered list without equal intervals between ranks.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Conjoint Analysis Practice Problems

cards.definition: The regression coefficient associated with a specific attribute level, representing that level's contribution to overall product preference relative to the baseline level.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Conjoint Analysis Practice Problems

cards.definition: The method by which respondents express product preferences in a conjoint study, including rank-ordering, rating scales, choice between pairs, and hybrid adaptive methods.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Conjoint Analysis Practice Problems

cards.definition: A specific product concept defined by one level for each attribute. In a full factorial design, the number of profiles equals the product of all attribute level counts.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Conjoint Analysis Practice Problems

cards.definition: A preference elicitation method in which respondents score each product profile on a numerical scale, allowing cardinal comparison of preferences.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Conjoint Analysis Practice Problems

cards.definition: The statistical technique used to estimate part-worth utilities in conjoint analysis. The dependent variable is the preference score or rank; independent variables are dummy-coded attribute levels.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Conjoint Analysis Practice Problems

cards.definition: The percentage of total utility variation attributable to each attribute, calculated as an attribute's part-worth range divided by the sum of all attributes' ranges.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Conjoint Analysis Practice Problems

cards.definition: An alternative name for conjoint analysis, emphasizing that respondents must implicitly trade off desirable attribute levels against undesirable ones when expressing preferences for product profiles.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Conjoint Analysis Practice Problems

cards.definition: The maximum additional price a consumer would pay to obtain an attribute improvement, estimated by dividing the attribute's part-worth by the price coefficient per dollar in the conjoint regression.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Conjoint Analysis Practice Problems

cards.definition: The full set of dimensions — perceptions, associations, expectations, and experiences — that differentiate a product in customers' minds beyond its physical or functional attributes.
The NPD stage in which multiple product concepts are created and explored before one is selected for further development.
The strategic act of differentiating a product through branding so that it competes on dimensions other than price alone, reducing substitutability.
Customer needs that are readily articulated and are typically already being addressed by existing market offerings.
The technical dimension of the Three Innovation Challenge — whether the organization has or can develop the capability to build and deliver the product.
A customer insight framework holding that customers "hire" products to accomplish a job, framed as "When [situation], I want to [motivation], so I can [desired outcome]."
Needs that customers may not be aware of or cannot yet express; addressing them can produce products that dramatically exceed expectations.
A clear, concise written expression of a customer need — written in "what" not "how," specific, positive, active voice, using "can" not "must/should," neutral and understandable.
The end-to-end sequence of organizational activities — from opportunity identification through launch — used to conceive, design, and commercialize a product.
The first stage of the NPD process, in which the organization identifies and selects among potential market needs or technology opportunities to pursue.

A portfolio risk-management approach in which a large initial pool of opportunities is progressively narrowed through multiple evidence-based filters until only the most promising opportunities remain.
A macro-environmental scanning framework covering Political, Economic, Social, Technological, Environmental, and Legal factors affecting an organization's general environment.

A competitive analysis framework examining Threat of New Entrants, Bargaining Power of Buyers, Bargaining Power of Suppliers, Threat of Substitutes, and Intensity of Rivalry in an operating environment.
Any offering — physical good, service, or other market offering — that can satisfy a want or need and is offered to a market for attention, acquisition, use, or consumption.
Quantified product requirements expressed as a metric, a target value, and units, derived from customer needs statements and prioritized by importance.
An opportunity evaluation framework testing whether a market opportunity is real, winnable, and worth it — mapping to desirability, competitive feasibility, and business viability respectively.
The requirement that a successful product simultaneously be desirable to people, viable for the business, and technically feasible — failure on any single dimension disqualifies the opportunity.
Needs that customers can easily express but that are not yet broadly addressed by available market offerings — representing a clear, validated opportunity space.
The business dimension of the Three Innovation Challenge — whether the product can generate sufficient revenue, margin, or strategic value to justify investment.

decks.title: Practice Problems: Project Management Practice Problems

cards.definition: The smallest unit of work effort consuming both time and resources that a project manager can schedule and control; a project step that takes time and uses resources.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Project Management Practice Problems

cards.definition: The maximum number of days an activity can be delayed without delaying the entire project; computed as LS − ES or LF − EF; zero for critical activities.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Project Management Practice Problems

cards.definition: The procedure of moving right to left through the network diagram to calculate Latest Finish and Latest Start for every activity, beginning from the project end date.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Project Management Practice Problems

cards.definition: The deliberate reduction of an activity's duration by adding extra resources or cost to shorten the critical path and reduce total project duration; only effective on critical activities.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Project Management Practice Problems

cards.definition: Any activity that lies on the critical path; it has zero slack and any delay to it directly delays the entire project by the same amount.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Project Management Practice Problems

cards.definition: The longest path from Start to Finish in a network diagram; determines the minimum possible project duration; activities on it have zero slack.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Project Management Practice Problems

cards.definition: A project scheduling technique that identifies all paths through a network diagram, determines the critical path, and computes earliest/latest start and finish times and slack for every activity.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Project Management Practice Problems

cards.definition: The estimated time required to complete one activity, measured in consistent units; the value shown in each node of the network diagram.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Project Management Practice Problems

cards.definition: The earliest possible day an activity can be completed, calculated as ES + Duration in the forward pass through the network.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Project Management Practice Problems

cards.definition: The earliest possible day an activity can begin, equal to the maximum Earliest Finish among all predecessors; Day 0 for activities with no predecessors.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Project Management Practice Problems

cards.definition: Another term for slack — the amount of scheduling flexibility available to an activity or path without delaying the project end date.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Project Management Practice Problems

cards.definition: The procedure of moving left to right through the network diagram to calculate Earliest Start and Earliest Finish for every activity, beginning from the project start.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Project Management Practice Problems

cards.definition: The latest day an activity can be completed without delaying the project; equal to the minimum Latest Start of all immediate successors; calculated in the backward pass.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Project Management Practice Problems

cards.definition: The latest day an activity can begin without delaying the project; calculated as LF − Duration in the backward pass.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Project Management Practice Problems

cards.definition: A directed graph representing all project activities and their precedence relationships; nodes are activities and arrows are dependencies; used to identify all paths and the critical path.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Project Management Practice Problems

cards.definition: A connecting point in a network diagram; in Activity-on-Node format, each node represents one activity and displays the activity label and duration.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Project Management Practice Problems

cards.definition: A sequence of activities leading continuously from the starting node to the finishing node of a network diagram; its length is the sum of its activities' durations.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Project Management Practice Problems

cards.definition: The difference between the critical path length and a given path's length; represents how much total delay that path can absorb before it becomes the new critical path.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Project Management Practice Problems

cards.definition: An activity that must be fully completed before a given activity can begin; shown in the predecessor column of an activity table and as an incoming arrow in a network diagram.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Project Management Practice Problems

cards.definition: The total time required to complete the entire project; equal to the length of the critical path; cannot be reduced without crashing one or more critical activities.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Project Management Practice Problems

decks.title: Supplementary Reading: Post-It Note Innovation History

cards.definition: The process of introducing a product so novel that it defines an entirely new market segment rather than competing within an existing one.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Post-It Note Innovation History

cards.definition: An individual within an organization who actively promotes and advocates for a new idea, technology, or product concept, often without formal authority or guaranteed reward.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Post-It Note Innovation History

cards.definition: The process by which ideas, technologies, or insights from one domain or department are transferred to another, often producing novel applications.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Post-It Note Innovation History

cards.definition: A cognitive strategy in which a product characteristic initially perceived as a flaw is reconceptualized as a benefit or core value proposition in a different context.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Post-It Note Innovation History

cards.definition: An innovation that creates a fundamentally new product category or radically changes how a need is met, as opposed to incremental improvements on existing products.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Post-It Note Innovation History

cards.definition: A sudden, often emotionally vivid insight in which a solution to a problem becomes clear.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Post-It Note Innovation History

cards.definition: The practice of promoting an idea or technology within an organization to build support, resources, and adoption, particularly when the idea lacks obvious commercial precedent.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Post-It Note Innovation History

cards.definition: A consumer need that is real and experienced but has not been consciously articulated or connected to a product solution.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Post-It Note Innovation History

cards.definition: Tiny spherical acrylic polymer beads developed by Spencer Silver in 1968 that form the core of the Post-it Note's adhesive, enabling contact stickiness without permanent bonding and clean repositioning.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Post-It Note Innovation History

cards.definition: The phase of new product development in which a firm recognizes an unmet or underserved need that could be addressed by a new product or service.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Post-It Note Innovation History

cards.definition: An adhesive that allows a bonded surface to be peeled away and reattached multiple times without losing tack or leaving residue.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Post-It Note Innovation History

cards.definition: An unexpected finding that arises from research aimed at a different goal, which nonetheless proves valuable. Requires researchers to remain observant when results diverge from expectations.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Post-It Note Innovation History

cards.definition: An innovation model in which a new technology is developed first and a market application is identified afterward, as opposed to demand-pull where a known customer need drives R&D.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Post-It Note Innovation History

cards.definition: Proprietary business information — including formulas, processes, or methods — that a company protects by keeping confidential rather than by filing a patent.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Post-It Note Innovation History

cards.definition: A pattern of product diffusion in which users naturally expose non-users to the product through ordinary use, triggering organic demand without paid advertising.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Post-It Note Innovation History

decks.title: Supplementary Reading: Product Concept Selection Methods

cards.definition: A qualitative comparison tool that maps expected benefits of a concept against the effort or resources required to execute it, helping teams identify high-impact, low-cost options.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Product Concept Selection Methods

cards.definition: A concept selection method in which the external customer or client holds final authority over which concept is chosen, regardless of internal analytical recommendations.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Product Concept Selection Methods

cards.definition: The practice of merging features or attributes from two or more concepts — including some eliminated in screening — into a new hybrid concept that captures the best elements of each.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Product Concept Selection Methods

cards.definition: A weighted quantitative decision tool in which each concept is rated numerically on each criterion, ratings are multiplied by criterion weights, and weighted scores are summed to rank concepts.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Product Concept Selection Methods

cards.definition: A rapid elimination tool in which concepts are compared against a reference (datum) using +/0/– ratings; concepts with the worst net scores are eliminated and the rest advance.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Product Concept Selection Methods

cards.definition: The reference concept used as the baseline in a Concept Screening Matrix; all other concepts are rated as better (+), the same (0), or worse (–) than the datum on each criterion.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Product Concept Selection Methods

cards.definition: The tendency for team members in open group settings to align their stated preferences with those of the most senior, vocal, or respected person present, distorting the true distribution of individual opinion.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Product Concept Selection Methods

cards.definition: A concept selection method in which the team deliberates until all members agree on or can accept a single choice; maximizes buy-in but is slow and can produce compromise solutions.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Product Concept Selection Methods

cards.definition: A psychological phenomenon in which the desire for harmony or conformity in a group results in irrational or suboptimal collective decision-making, with minority viewpoints suppressed.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Product Concept Selection Methods

cards.definition: A concept selection method in which a designated authority figure makes the final selection, typically after receiving team input; efficient but potentially exclusionary.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Product Concept Selection Methods

cards.definition: An external validation method in which product concepts are exposed to real potential customers through experiments, surveys, or pilots, and customer response data informs the selection decision.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Product Concept Selection Methods

cards.definition: In the Concept Screening Matrix, the result of subtracting the count of minus (–) ratings from the count of plus (+) ratings for a given concept; used to rank concepts for advancement or elimination.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Product Concept Selection Methods

cards.definition: A concept evaluation approach in which concepts are posted to an online audience for feedback, ratings, or comments; scalable and fast but potentially shallow and unrepresentative of the target market.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Product Concept Selection Methods

cards.definition: A concept selection technique in which team members publicly vote for their preferred concepts; fast and energizing but susceptible to groupthink and dominance effects.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Product Concept Selection Methods

cards.definition: A qualitative evaluation tool that organizes the advantages and disadvantages of each concept side by side, enabling straightforward comparison without numerical scoring.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Product Concept Selection Methods

cards.definition: The generic name for the two-stage concept selection system developed by Stuart Pugh, comprising a Concept Screening Matrix (stage one) and a Concept Scoring Matrix (stage two).
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Product Concept Selection Methods

cards.definition: An anonymous voting method in which participants record preferences privately before results are tallied, removing social pressure and hierarchy bias from the selection process.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Product Concept Selection Methods

cards.definition: A check performed after completing the Concept Scoring Matrix in which criterion weights or ratings are varied to determine whether plausible changes would alter which concept ranks first.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Product Concept Selection Methods

cards.definition: The product of a criterion's importance weight and a concept's raw rating on that criterion; weighted scores for all criteria are summed to produce a concept's total score in the Concept Scoring Matrix.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Product Concept Selection Methods
